note: First bodyBlock is the <aside> system-note. ALL currency figures kept 1:1; only labels/headers/context cells translated.

fields.title: Grand Slam Prize Money Breakdown — 2026 Tables (AO/RG/W/USO) | SUPER.TENNIS
fields.metaDescription: 2026 Grand Slam prize money: singles champion checks, per-round prize money, total prize pools at the Australian Open, Roland Garros, Wimbledon, and US Open. Doubles + qualifying + wheelchair amounts included.
fields.quickAnswer: 2026 Grand Slam prize money — singles champion checks: Australian Open A$3.5M (~US$2.3M), Roland Garros €2.4M, Wimbledon £3.0M (~US$3.8M), US Open ~$3.6M. Total prize pools: Australian Open A$96.5M (largest in tennis history), US Open ~$75M, Wimbledon £53M, Roland Garros €58M. Even first-round losers earn $60-100K. Equal men's and women's prize money has been standard since 2007 (US Open since 1973, Australian Open since 2001).
bodyBlocks:
Current System Note

Per-round figures reflect 2026 confirmed amounts as announced by each Slam ~3-4 weeks before its event. Exact dollar conversions vary day-to-day with exchange rates — figures here use approximate end-of-2025 conversions. Each Slam publishes its prize money breakdown on its official website.

EventTotal Prize MoneyDatesNotes
Australian Open 2026A$96.5M (~$63M)JanuaryLargest total prize pool in tennis history
Roland Garros 2026€58M (~$63M)May-JuneIncludes singles, doubles, mixed, wheelchair
Wimbledon 2026£53M (~$67M)June-July~£25M to singles main draw
US Open 2026~$75MAugust-SeptemberLargest in USD terms among Slams
All four Slams pay equal amounts to men's and women's singles winners (since 2007). Below: approximate per-round figures in each Slam's native currency.

Round ReachedAO 🇦🇺RG 🇫🇷Wim 🇬🇧USO 🇺🇸
ChampionA$3.5M€2.4M£3.0M$3.6M
FinalistA$1.9M€1.27M£1.52M$1.8M
SemifinalistA$1.1M€780K£775K$1.0M
QuarterfinalistA$665K€440K£440K$530K
Round of 16A$405K€265K£265K$325K
Round of 32A$255K€158K£158K$215K
Round of 64A$170K€100K£105K$140K
Round of 128 (first-round)A$120K€78K£66K$100K
Note: qualifying-round prize money (for players competing to enter the main draw) is paid separately: ~$30-50K to qualifying-draw winners across all four Slams.
Beyond Singles: Where the Rest of the Pool Goes
Singles draws absorb the majority of each Slam's prize pool — but not all of it. The remaining ~30% is split across:

CategoryShare of PoolDetail
Singles~70%128 main draw + 128 qualifying. Champion gets the biggest single check.
Doubles~12-15%Champion teams typically earn $700K-$1M per Slam (split between the two players).
Mixed doubles~2-3%Champion team typically earns $200K-$400K combined.
Qualifying singles~5-7%Players who reach main draw via qualifying earn $25-50K just for the run.
Wheelchair singles + doubles~3-5%Equal prize money increases each year; now well above $500K for singles champions.
Tracking the Wimbledon and US Open singles champion's check across the Open Era shows how dramatically the sport's economics have grown:

YearWimbledon ChampionUS Open ChampionEra
1968£2,000$14,000Open Era begins
1980£20,000$46,000Borg-McEnroe era
1990£230,000$350,000Sampras era
2000£477,500$800,000Pete Sampras 7th Wimbledon
2010£1.0M$1.7MFederer-Nadal era peak
2015£1.88M$3.3MBig Three dominance
2020— (canceled)$3.0MCOVID year
2025£3.0M$3.6MModern figures
2026£3.0M~$3.6M+Current — confirmed by tournaments

From 1968 (£2,000 at Wimbledon) to 2026 (£3,000,000), the champion's check has grown roughly 1,500-fold. Even adjusting for inflation (£2,000 in 1968 ≈ £40,000 in 2026), the real-terms increase is about 75×. The drivers: global TV deals, sponsorship inflation, women's sports investment growth, and competitive pressure between the four Slams to maintain prestige.
How Tennis Prize Money Compares to Other Sports
Tennis is one of the most lucrative individual sports for the top players, but compared to team sports it's more uneven:

• Golf majors — Masters 2026 champion: ~$3.6M; PGA Championship: ~$3.3M; comparable to tennis Slams.
  • Formula 1 — top drivers earn $40-70M/year in base salary alone, but FIA prize money is far smaller. F1 economics are more team-based.
  • Boxing pay-per-view — major fights can pay $50-150M+ per bout. Tennis Slams pay less per event but happen four times a year for every player who qualifies.
  • NBA / NFL / Premier League — top players earn $50-100M+ salary (without prize money structures). Tennis's prize-money model is more meritocratic but less consistent.

faqs.q: Which Grand Slam has the largest prize money?
faqs.a: In 2026, the Australian Open has the largest total prize pool at A$96.5 million (approximately US$63M) — the largest in tennis history. The US Open is largest in USD terms at ~$75M total. Roland Garros: €58M (~$63M). Wimbledon: £53M (~$67M). All four pay similar amounts to the singles champion (~$2.3-3.6M); differences come from how the total is distributed across doubles, qualifying, and wheelchair.

faqs.q: How much does a Wimbledon champion earn in 2026?
faqs.a: The 2026 Wimbledon singles champion receives £3.0 million (approximately $3.8M at current exchange rates). This is identical for men's and women's singles winners (equal prize money since 2007). Doubles champion teams earn approximately £700K total (£350K per player). Junior champions earn £30K. Wheelchair singles champions earn £75K+.

faqs.q: What's the US Open prize money for 2026?
faqs.a: The 2026 US Open singles champion is expected to earn approximately $3.6M+ (the exact figure is confirmed by the USTA ~6 weeks before the event each year). Total prize pool: ~$75M, the largest in USD terms among the four Slams. The US Open has consistently led other Slams in prize money increases, often by 5-8% year-over-year.

faqs.q: How much does a first-round loser at a Grand Slam earn?
faqs.a: 2026 first-round prize money at each Slam: Australian Open A$120K (~$78K), Roland Garros €78K (~$85K), Wimbledon £66K (~$83K), US Open ~$100K. Players who lose in the first round still take home meaningful money — designed to make competing at Slams financially viable for players who travel long distances and have significant operating costs.

faqs.q: When did Grand Slam prize money first exceed $1M for the singles champion?
faqs.a: The US Open was the first Slam to pay $1M+ to the singles champion, reaching $1M in 2002 (men's, women's became equal soon after at the higher figure). Wimbledon followed in 2003 (men's; women's reached parity in 2007). Australian Open reached A$1M in 2004. Roland Garros reached €1M in 2006. All four Slams have continued increasing the champion's purse roughly 5-10% per year since.

faqs.q: Why does Australian Open have such a high total prize pool?
faqs.a: The Australian Open's 2026 total prize pool of A$96.5M is the largest because of: (1) aggressive prize money increases at lower rounds — AO pays more to first-round losers than other Slams; (2) expansion of doubles and wheelchair prize money; (3) currency effects — A$ totals look larger than the equivalent USD amount; (4) strong title sponsorship from Asian-Pacific brands. In US-dollar terms (~$63M), it's slightly below the US Open's ~$75M.

faqs.q: Do tennis players get bonus money for winning multiple Slams in a year?
faqs.a: Not directly from the Slams themselves — each Slam pays its own prize money independently. However, some sponsorship deals include Slam-win bonuses. For example, racket and apparel sponsors typically pay $500K-$2M bonuses for a Slam title. Additionally, the player earns 2,000 ranking points per Slam title — affecting endorsement deal renegotiation at year-end. Career Grand Slam winners (winning all four in a career) often receive additional sponsor bonuses too.
